claimed_contribution: Provides controlled experimental evidence on entertainment-education placements, which the authors note had little prior controlled experimental testing.

Shows that when messages are salient, smoker-disapproval messages can dominate indirectly competing smoker-attractiveness and smoker-prevalence messages, and identifies a boundary condition when direct smoker approval is also present.
Demonstrates that an epilogue disclosure can activate persuasion knowledge and backfire for counterattitudinal viewers (smokers), informing disclosure and persuasion-knowledge theory.
context: The studies are set in entertainment education, using antismoking content embedded in edited versions of the TV comedy Clueless (the "Model Smoker" episode). Participants were U.S. ninth-grade students recruited through public high schools.
design_and_data: Study 1 used a 3 x 2 between-subjects experiment with 1046 ninth graders from eight U.S. public high schools, randomly assigned to message versions with (a) attractiveness+prevalence+disapproval smoking content, (b) attractiveness+prevalence content, or (c) no-smoking control, crossed with prior smoking status. Study 2 used a 3 x 2 x 2 between-subjects experiment with 1804 ninth graders from nine public high schools, varying message version (including a directly competing approval+disapproval version), epilogue vs no epilogue, and prior smoking status. Outcomes included coded thought listings, smoking-related beliefs, smoking intent, recall and valence checks, suspicion, and persuasion knowledge; Study 2 also collected prior smoking beliefs in a pretest one month earlier.
main_findings: Across both experiments, the version with smoker attractiveness, prevalence, and disapproval messages increased smoker-disapproval thoughts and disapproval beliefs versus control, indicating dominance of disapproval over indirectly competing positive cues.
When a directly competing smoker-approval message was added, deterrence was nullified on key outcomes: disapproval beliefs and smoker intent were no longer improved versus control.

The epilogue greatly increased persuasion knowledge and produced a boomerang effect among smokers by weakening disapproval beliefs and increasing smoking intent; nonsmokers did not show this adverse pattern.
one_line_summary: In two experiments with 2850 adolescents viewing edited television content, the paper finds that antismoking disapproval messages can beat indirect prosmoking cues, but direct approval cues and disclosure epilogues can eliminate or reverse deterrence effects.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior social-norm and reference-group message research by experimentally testing salient indirect and direct message competition in an entertainment-education setting. It also refines persuasion-knowledge and disclosure findings by showing that counterattitudinal audience-message alignment can drive boomerang responses even with delayed disclosure.
research_gap: Prior evidence on competing reference-group messages was inconsistent and often did not ensure message salience, and educational placements in entertainment media had little controlled experimental evidence. The paper also points to limited research on directly competing approval-disapproval messages and on whether disclosure epilogues help or backfire.
research_question: How do adolescents respond to entertainment-education placements that combine competing reference-group messages about smoking? The paper tests the net effects of indirectly competing prevalence/attractiveness versus disapproval messages, directly competing approval versus disapproval messages, and whether epilogue disclosures of persuasive intent alter responses through persuasion knowledge.
